← All guides
Mindfulness & Regulation Skills
Personal & Professional Resilience — Self-Help Guide Series
"You cannot pour from an empty cup."
Working in healthcare is meaningful, demanding, and emotionally intensive. Over time, the accumulation of pressure, pace, and responsibility can leave us feeling depleted, anxious, or disconnected — even when we love what we do.

These skills are simple, evidence-based, and available to you at any moment — including right now. Use what helps. Return to what works. Skip what doesn't feel right for you today.

Your window of tolerance — tap each zone to learn more

Hyperarousal — too much
Panic, anxiety, overwhelm, anger, racing thoughts, hypervigilance
Window of tolerance — just right
Calm, present, able to think clearly, connected, resourceful
Hypoarousal — too little
Numbness, shutdown, disconnection, fatigue, emptiness, fog
🌱
Grounding
5-4-3-2-1 guided
🌬️
Breathing
Box, 4-7-8, sigh
🧘
Body scan
5-minute guided
🧠
Emotions
STOP + vocabulary
☀️
Mindfulness
3-minute space
📋
My plan
Personal choices
🌱
Grounding techniques

Grounding techniques shift your attention away from distressing thoughts and back into the here and now — your body, your senses, your immediate physical environment. Especially useful during anxiety, overwhelm, or racing thoughts.

Why this helps: Grounding activates the prefrontal cortex — the thinking brain — which calms the amygdala (the alarm centre). By anchoring yourself in sensory experience, you interrupt the stress spiral and signal safety to your nervous system.
The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding method — tap each step as you complete it
✓ Well done — take one slow breath and notice how you feel compared to when you started.
✓
5 THINGS YOU CAN SEE
Look carefully around you — notice colours, shapes, light, shadows, and textures you might normally overlook.
✓
4 THINGS YOU CAN TOUCH
Feel the texture, temperature, and weight of things near you — your chair, your clothing, a surface.
✓
3 THINGS YOU CAN HEAR
Listen for sounds both nearby and in the distance — close your eyes if it helps you focus.
✓
2 THINGS YOU CAN SMELL
Or two things you like the smell of — real or remembered. Even the air around you counts.
✓
1 THING YOU CAN TASTE
Even the taste of the air or a recent drink counts. Take a moment to notice it fully.
Feet on the floor
1
Sit upright with both feet flat on the floor.
2
Press gently downward and feel the ground beneath you.
3
Say to yourself: "I am here. I am safe. I am present."
4
Breathe slowly for 60 seconds.
Cold water reset
1
Run cold water over your wrists or splash your face.
2
Focus entirely on the sensation of the temperature.
3
This activates the dive reflex, slowing heart rate quickly.
4
Breathe steadily as you do this.
▸ Reflect & notice
When do you most need a grounding technique? What are your early warning signals?
Which of these techniques felt most natural or accessible to you?
Where in your day could you practise grounding, even briefly?
🌬️
Breathing for regulation
Background ambient — Voxscape
Eugenio Mininni · Mixkit Free License · loops while you practise
🔈

Your breath is the one part of the autonomic nervous system you can consciously control — and this makes it your most immediately accessible regulation tool. Making your exhale longer than your inhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system.

Why this helps: Research shows that slow, controlled breathing reduces cortisol, lowers blood pressure, and improves cognitive performance. It is one of the fastest evidence-based interventions for acute stress.
Interactive breathing guide — choose a technique and follow the circle
Used by military and emergency services worldwide. Inhale for 4 — hold for 4 — exhale for 4 — hold for 4. Repeat 4 cycles.
Ready
—
Press start to begin
Cycles completed: 0
▸ Reflect & notice
When do you notice that your breathing becomes shallow or rapid?
Which breathing technique felt most accessible to you? Why?
Could you set a reminder once a day this week to practise one of these techniques?
🧘
Body awareness & scan
Guided body scan — press play and close your eyes
10-minute voice meditation · courtesy of Fostering Resilience
🔈

Much of our stress and emotion is stored not in our thoughts, but in our bodies — in tension, tightness, fatigue, or discomfort. A body scan trains you to notice these physical experiences with curiosity rather than judgement.

Why this helps: Research by Jon Kabat-Zinn and others shows that body scan practices significantly reduce symptoms of anxiety, chronic pain, and exhaustion when practised regularly — even for as little as ten minutes.
5-minute body scan — step-by-step guided experience
Find a comfortable position — seated or lying down. Close your eyes or soften your gaze. Press Start scan when you are ready.
▸ Reflect & notice
Where in your body do you tend to hold stress or tension most? (Jaw? Shoulders? Stomach?)
Were there any areas you had difficulty feeling — or areas that surprised you?
What do you think your body has been trying to tell you lately?
🧠
Emotional regulation skills

Emotions are not problems to be solved — they are signals to be understood. Emotional regulation is not about suppressing what you feel; it is about developing the capacity to experience feelings without being overwhelmed, and to choose how you respond.

Why this helps: Dr. Dan Siegel's research shows that labelling emotions with precise language reduces activity in the amygdala and increases prefrontal regulation — naming your feelings literally calms your brain. This is sometimes called "name it to tame it".
The STOP technique — creating a pause
S
Stop
Whatever you are doing, pause for a moment. Just stop.
T
Take a breath
One slow, deliberate breath in and out. Feel it fully.
O
Observe
What is happening right now — in your body, your thoughts, your emotions? Just notice.
P
Proceed
Choose your next action with intention rather than react from impulse.
Expanding your emotional vocabulary — tap any word to explore its meaning
The more precisely you can name it, the more you can regulate it.
Instead of "stressed" — try:
overwhelmed
pressured
stretched
depleted
flooded
frantic
fragile
Instead of "angry" — try:
frustrated
resentful
irritated
indignant
hurt
betrayed
powerless
Instead of "sad" — try:
grieving
disappointed
lonely
despondent
melancholic
disheartened
Instead of "fine" — try:
settled
content
calm
energised
grounded
hopeful
connected
▸ Reflect & notice
What emotions do you find it hardest to sit with? What do you usually do with them?
Think of a recent emotional reaction. Using the vocabulary above, can you name it more precisely?
What would it mean for you to respond to that emotion rather than react to it?
☀️
Everyday mindfulness

Mindfulness is not meditation — although meditation is one way to practise it. At its simplest, mindfulness is paying attention to your present experience with openness and without judgement. It can be practised in the shower, on a commute, eating lunch, or walking between wards.

Why this helps: Even brief mindfulness moments — as short as 90 seconds — can interrupt habitual stress patterns and reset your physiological state. The key is intention and repetition, not duration.
The 3-minute breathing space (MBCT)
1
Arrive — minute 1
Ask yourself: "What is here right now?" Notice your thoughts, feelings, and body sensations without trying to change them.
2
Gather — minute 2
Bring your full attention to the physical sensation of your breathing. Notice each inhale and exhale. When your mind wanders, gently return.
3
Expand — minute 3
Widen your awareness outward from your breath to your whole body, then to the room, then to sounds around you. Carry this into the next activity.
Mindful micro-moments
1
One mindful cup of tea or coffee — no phone, just taste and warmth.
2
Three conscious breaths before entering a room.
3
Pause at the threshold of a door. Notice what you are carrying.
4
Look at the sky for 30 seconds without thinking.
5
Notice five sounds during a walk or commute.
The mindful check-in

Three times a day, pause and ask yourself:

1
How is my body right now?
2
How is my mood right now?
3
What do I need most in this moment?
4
Then offer yourself one small thing in response.
▸ Reflect & notice
What one mindful micro-moment could you realistically build into each day this week?
When in your day do you feel most disconnected from the present moment?
If you could offer yourself one compassionate thought right now, what would it be?
📋
My personal regulation plan

Looking back through this guide, note your personal choices below. Use this as your anchor — return to it, revise it, and share it with someone who can support you.

e.g. 5-4-3-2-1 grounding, box breathing, cold water reset…
e.g. movement, cold water, physiological sigh, connecting with someone…
e.g. 3-minute breathing space after lunch, mindful cup of tea each morning…
e.g. name and relationship…
✓ Copied!

When you need more support

These techniques are powerful tools — and they are not a substitute for professional support when you need it. If difficult feelings are frequent, persistent, or significantly affecting your daily life, please reach out.

⚕Your GP or doctor — your first point of contact for mental and physical health concerns
⚕A qualified mental health practitioner — a counsellor, psychologist, or therapist trained to support you
♥A trusted friend, family member, or colleague — someone who will listen with care and without judgement
You have taken time for yourself today. That matters.
Every breath taken with awareness is a small act of resilience.
